content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 16, 1992

Mr. Clarence J. Turner
201 Paularino Ave.
Costa Mesa, CA 92626

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-92-350

Dear Mr. Turner:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your
question seeks general guidance, we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

What are the reporting requirements of an organization that
plans to circulate a petition?

CONCLUSION

The organization will incur campaign filing obligations as a
committee if it receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in
a calendar year for the purpose of qualifying a measure for the
ballot.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act requires all committees supporting
or opposing California state and local candidates and the
qualification or passage of state and local ballot measures to

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Clarence J. Turner
I-92-350
Page 2


file campaign statements disclosing contributions received and
expenditures made.  (Section 84100, et seq.)

     Section 82013 defines "committee" as any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the following:

          (a)  Receives contributions totaling one
     thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
     year; or

          (b)  Makes independent expenditures totaling
     one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
     year; or

          (c)  Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
     dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at
     the behest of candidates or committees.

     Sections 82015 and 82025 define "contribution" and
"expenditure" in part as payments which are made for political
purposes.  A payment is made for "political purposes" if it is for
the purpose of supporting or opposing officeholders, candidates,
or the qualification or passage of any measure.  (Regulations
18215 and 18225.)

     Section 82043 defines "measure" as any constitutional amendment or other proposition which is submitted to a popular vote at
an election by action of a legislative body, or which is submitted
or is intended to be submitted to a popular vote at an election by
initiative, referendum or recall procedure whether or not it
qualifies for the ballot.

     Your organization will incur campaign disclosure filing
obligations if it receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more
in a calendar year.  To determine if $1,000 has been received, you
must count all monetary and non-monetary contributions received,
including loans and any personal funds of your own spent in connection with the qualification effort.

     Section 84101(a) requires every committee qualified under
Section 82013(a) to file a Statement of Organization (Form 410)
with the Secretary of State within 10 days after receiving
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year.  In addition to filing the original and one copy of Form 410 with the
Secretary of State, the committee must file a copy with the local
filing officer with whom it will file the originals of its
campaign reports.

     Once the committee qualifies under Section 82013(a), it will
also be required to file periodic campaign reports (Form 419 or
450).  If the organization becomes a "committee" during the period
in which it is attempting to qualify the measure, the committee
must file a "post qualification" campaign report due 21 days after

Clarence J. Turner
I-92-350
Page 3


the deadline for filing petitions.  The closing date for the
period covered by this report is seven days prior to the date it
is required to be filed.  (Section 84200.5(f).)  The committee
also will be required to file semi-annual campaign statements each
year and, if the measure qualifies for the ballot, pre-election
statements must be filed prior to the election.  (Sections 84200,
84200.5.)

     Semi-annual statements are due no later than July 31 for the
period ending June 30, and no later than January 31 for the period
ending December 31.  (Section 84200.)  Campaign statements must be
filed until the committee is eligible to terminate.  (Sections
82013, 84214.)  A committee primarily formed to support or oppose
the qualification of a city measure would file the required
campaign disclosure statements (Form 419 or 450) with the city
clerk.

     Enclosed you will find Manual D--Campaign Manual for Committees Primarily Formed to Support or Oppose the Qualification, Passage or Defeat of a Ballot Measure.

     If you have any questions concerning this advice, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Assistant General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Sandra L. Taylor
                                             Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure
